categories.name: Data Structures & Algorithms
categories.description: Practice DSA questions for technical interviews

categories.name: System Design
categories.description: Learn system design concepts and patterns

questions.question: What is the time complexity of binary search?
questions.explanation: Binary search divides the search space in half with each iteration, resulting in O(log n) time complexity.

questions.question: Which data structure uses LIFO (Last In First Out) principle?
questions.explanation: A Stack follows the LIFO principle where the last element added is the first one to be removed.

questions.question: What is the worst-case time complexity of QuickSort?
questions.explanation: QuickSort has a worst-case time complexity of O(n²) when the pivot selection is poor, though average case is O(n log n).

questions.question: Which traversal visits nodes in the order: Left, Root, Right?
questions.explanation: Inorder traversal visits the left subtree first, then the root, and finally the right subtree.

questions.question: What is the space complexity of merge sort?
questions.explanation: Merge sort requires O(n) additional space for the temporary arrays used during merging.

questions.question: Which data structure is best for implementing a priority queue?
questions.explanation: A Heap (typically a binary heap) is the most efficient data structure for implementing a priority queue with O(log n) insertion and deletion.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of accessing an element in a hash table (average case)?
questions.explanation: Hash tables provide O(1) average-case time complexity for insertion, deletion, and lookup operations.

questions.question: Which algorithm is used to find the shortest path in a weighted graph?
questions.explanation: Dijkstra's Algorithm finds the shortest path from a source vertex to all other vertices in a weighted graph with non-negative weights.

questions.question: What is a balanced binary search tree?
options: A tree where all leaves are at the same level
A tree where the height difference between left and right subtrees is at most 1
A tree with equal number of nodes on left and right
A tree sorted in ascending order
questions.explanation: A balanced BST maintains a height difference of at most 1 between left and right subtrees for all nodes, ensuring O(log n) operations.

questions.question: Which sorting algorithm is most efficient for nearly sorted data?
questions.explanation: Insertion Sort performs very well on nearly sorted data with O(n) time complexity in the best case.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a sentinel node in a linked list?
options: To mark the end of the list
questions.explanation: A sentinel (dummy) node simplifies code by eliminating special cases for empty lists and boundary conditions.

questions.question: Which data structure would you use to implement undo functionality?
questions.explanation: A Stack is ideal for undo functionality as it follows LIFO - the most recent action is undone first.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of building a heap from an unsorted array?
questions.explanation: Building a heap from an unsorted array using the heapify process takes O(n) time, not O(n log n) as commonly assumed.

questions.question: Which algorithm detects cycles in a linked list?
questions.explanation: Floyd's Cycle Detection (Tortoise and Hare) uses two pointers moving at different speeds to detect cycles in O(n) time and O(1) space.

questions.question: What is the maximum number of nodes in a binary tree of height h?
questions.explanation: A complete binary tree of height h has a maximum of 2^(h+1) - 1 nodes.

questions.question: Which data structure is used in BFS traversal?
questions.explanation: BFS (Breadth-First Search) uses a Queue to process nodes level by level in FIFO order.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of deleting a node from the middle of a doubly linked list?
questions.explanation: If you have a pointer to the node, deletion in a doubly linked list is O(1) as you can update prev and next pointers directly.

questions.question: Which algorithm is used for topological sorting?
questions.explanation: Topological sorting can be implemented using both DFS (with recursion) and BFS (Kahn's algorithm).

questions.question: What is a trie data structure primarily used for?
questions.explanation: A Trie (prefix tree) is optimized for string operations like autocomplete, spell checking, and prefix matching.

questions.question: What is the space complexity of recursive DFS?
questions.explanation: Recursive DFS uses the call stack, requiring O(h) space where h is the maximum depth/height of recursion.

questions.question: Which sorting algorithm is stable and has O(n log n) worst-case complexity?
questions.explanation: Merge Sort is stable (preserves relative order of equal elements) and guarantees O(n log n) in all cases.

questions.question: What is the minimum number of nodes in an AVL tree of height h?
questions.explanation: The minimum number of nodes in an AVL tree of height h follows the Fibonacci sequence: F(h+2) - 1.

questions.question: Which algorithm finds the Minimum Spanning Tree?
questions.explanation: Both Kruskal's and Prim's algorithms find the Minimum Spanning Tree of a weighted undirected graph.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of finding an element in a balanced BST?
questions.explanation: In a balanced BST, search operations take O(log n) time due to the tree's balanced height.

questions.question: Which data structure is best for implementing LRU cache?
questions.explanation: LRU cache is efficiently implemented using a Hash Map for O(1) lookup and a Doubly Linked List for O(1) insertion/deletion.

questions.question: What is dynamic programming?
questions.explanation: Dynamic Programming is an optimization technique that solves complex problems by breaking them down and storing solutions to subproblems.

questions.question: Which algorithm is used to find strongly connected components?
questions.explanation: Kosaraju's Algorithm (or Tarjan's) finds strongly connected components in a directed graph using two DFS passes.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of counting sort?
questions.explanation: Counting sort runs in O(n + k) time where n is the number of elements and k is the range of input values.

questions.question: Which data structure uses FIFO principle?
questions.explanation: A Queue follows the FIFO (First In First Out) principle where the first element added is the first one removed.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the master theorem?
questions.explanation: The Master Theorem provides a way to analyze the time complexity of divide-and-conquer algorithms.

questions.question: Which operation is NOT efficient in an array?
questions.explanation: Inserting at the beginning of an array requires shifting all elements, making it O(n) operation.

questions.question: What is a circular queue?
options: A queue where rear connects to front
questions.explanation: A circular queue connects the rear position back to the front, efficiently utilizing array space.

questions.question: Which algorithm solves the all-pairs shortest path problem?
questions.explanation: Floyd-Warshall algorithm finds shortest paths between all pairs of vertices in O(n³) time.

questions.question: What is the height of a complete binary tree with n nodes?
questions.explanation: A complete binary tree with n nodes has height ⌊log₂(n)⌋ (floor of log base 2 of n).

questions.question: Which data structure is used in expression evaluation?
questions.explanation: Stack is used to evaluate postfix expressions and convert between infix, prefix, and postfix notations.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of the Bellman-Ford algorithm?
questions.explanation: Bellman-Ford algorithm runs in O(V * E) time where V is vertices and E is edges.

questions.question: Which tree traversal uses a stack implicitly?
questions.explanation: Recursive tree traversals (preorder, inorder, postorder) use the call stack implicitly.

questions.question: What is a deque?
questions.explanation: A Deque (Double-Ended Queue) allows insertion and deletion at both front and rear ends.

questions.question: Which sorting algorithm has the best best-case time complexity?
questions.explanation: Insertion Sort has O(n) best-case complexity when the array is already sorted.

questions.question: What is the space complexity of iterative DFS using a stack?
questions.explanation: Iterative DFS using an explicit stack requires O(V) space in the worst case where V is the number of vertices.

questions.question: Which data structure is best for implementing a graph?
questions.explanation: Graphs are typically represented using Adjacency Lists (for sparse graphs) or Adjacency Matrices (for dense graphs).

questions.question: What is the time complexity of heap sort?
questions.explanation: Heap Sort has O(n log n) time complexity in all cases (best, average, and worst).

questions.question: Which algorithm is greedy?
questions.explanation: Dijkstra's Algorithm is a greedy algorithm that always selects the vertex with minimum distance.

questions.question: What is a self-balancing tree?
options: A tree that never needs balancing
A tree that automatically maintains balance after operations
questions.explanation: Self-balancing trees (like AVL, Red-Black) automatically maintain balance after insertions and deletions.

questions.question: Which data structure is used in recursion?
questions.explanation: Recursion uses the call stack to store function calls and local variables.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of radix sort?
questions.explanation: Radix sort runs in O(d * (n + k)) where d is the number of digits, n is the number of elements, and k is the range.

questions.question: Which algorithm uses divide and conquer?
questions.explanation: Merge Sort divides the array into halves, recursively sorts them, and merges the results.

questions.question: What is a binary heap property?
options: All leaves are at same level
questions.explanation: In a max heap, parent is greater than children; in a min heap, parent is smaller than children.

questions.question: Which algorithm detects negative weight cycles?
questions.explanation: Bellman-Ford algorithm can detect negative weight cycles in a graph.

questions.question: What is memoization?
options: Caching function results to avoid recomputation
questions.explanation: Memoization is an optimization technique that stores the results of expensive function calls and returns cached results for same inputs.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a Load Balancer?
questions.explanation: A Load Balancer distributes incoming network traffic across multiple servers to ensure no single server is overwhelmed.

questions.question: Which database type is best for handling hierarchical data?
questions.explanation: Graph databases are specifically designed to handle hierarchical and interconnected data efficiently.

questions.question: What does CAP theorem stand for?
questions.explanation: CAP theorem states that a distributed system can only guarantee two out of three: Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance.

questions.question: What is horizontal scaling?
options: Adding more power to existing servers
Adding more servers to the system
questions.explanation: Horizontal scaling (scale-out) means adding more machines to your pool of resources.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a CDN?
options: Distribute content geographically closer to users
questions.explanation: A CDN (Content Delivery Network) caches content at edge locations to reduce latency and improve load times.

questions.question: Which caching strategy updates cache when data is written?
questions.explanation: Write-through caching writes data to both cache and database simultaneously, ensuring consistency.

questions.question: What is database sharding?
questions.explanation: Sharding is a horizontal partitioning strategy that distributes data across multiple database instances.

questions.question: What is eventual consistency?
options: Data becomes consistent after some time
questions.explanation: Eventual consistency means that given enough time without updates, all replicas will converge to the same value.

questions.question: Which protocol is used for real-time bidirectional communication?
questions.explanation: WebSocket provides full-duplex communication channels over a single TCP connection, ideal for real-time applications.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a message queue?
options: Decouple services and enable asynchronous communication
questions.explanation: Message queues enable asynchronous communication between services, improving scalability and fault tolerance.

questions.question: What is a microservices architecture?
questions.explanation: Microservices architecture structures an application as a collection of loosely coupled, independently deployable services.

questions.question: What is the purpose of an API Gateway?
options: Single entry point for all client requests
questions.explanation: An API Gateway acts as a reverse proxy, routing requests, aggregating responses, and handling cross-cutting concerns.

questions.question: Which database is best for time-series data?
questions.explanation: InfluxDB and similar time-series databases are optimized for storing and querying time-stamped data.

questions.question: What is database replication?
options: Copying data to multiple database instances
questions.explanation: Database replication involves copying data from one database to one or more databases for redundancy and availability.

questions.question: What is the purpose of rate limiting?
options: Control the rate of requests to prevent abuse
questions.explanation: Rate limiting controls the number of requests a user can make in a given time period to prevent abuse and ensure fair usage.

questions.question: What is a reverse proxy?
options: A server that sits in front of web servers
questions.explanation: A reverse proxy sits in front of web servers and forwards client requests to the appropriate backend server.

questions.question: Which consistency model does Amazon DynamoDB use?
questions.explanation: DynamoDB offers both eventual consistency (default) and strong consistency options for read operations.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a circuit breaker pattern?
options: Prevent cascading failures in distributed systems
questions.explanation: Circuit breaker pattern prevents an application from repeatedly trying to execute an operation that's likely to fail.

questions.question: What is database indexing?
options: Creating data structures to improve query performance
questions.explanation: Database indexes are data structures that improve the speed of data retrieval operations at the cost of additional storage.

questions.question: What is the difference between SQL and NoSQL databases?
options: SQL uses structured schema, NoSQL is schema-less or flexible
questions.explanation: SQL databases use structured schemas and ACID transactions, while NoSQL databases offer flexible schemas and horizontal scalability.

questions.question: What is a distributed hash table (DHT)?
options: A decentralized distributed system for key-value storage
questions.explanation: DHT is a distributed system that provides a lookup service similar to a hash table, used in P2P systems.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a service mesh?
questions.explanation: A service mesh provides infrastructure layer for handling service-to-service communication, including load balancing, encryption, and observability.

questions.question: What is database normalization?
questions.explanation: Normalization is the process of organizing data in a database to reduce redundancy and improve data integrity.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Kafka?
questions.explanation: Apache Kafka is a distributed event streaming platform used for building real-time data pipelines and streaming applications.

questions.question: What is vertical scaling?
options: Adding more resources to existing server
questions.explanation: Vertical scaling (scale-up) means adding more power (CPU, RAM) to an existing machine.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Redis?
options: In-memory data structure store for caching
questions.explanation: Redis is an in-memory data structure store used as a database, cache, and message broker.

questions.question: What is the two-phase commit protocol?
questions.explanation: Two-phase commit is a distributed algorithm that coordinates all processes participating in a distributed atomic transaction.

questions.question: What is database denormalization?
options: Adding redundancy to improve read performance
questions.explanation: Denormalization adds redundancy to a database to improve read performance at the cost of write performance and storage.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Elasticsearch?
questions.explanation: Elasticsearch is a distributed search and analytics engine built on Apache Lucene, used for full-text search.

questions.question: What is the Saga pattern?
options: A pattern for managing distributed transactions
questions.explanation: Saga pattern manages distributed transactions by breaking them into a sequence of local transactions with compensating actions.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a bloom filter?
options: Probabilistic data structure to test set membership
questions.explanation: A bloom filter is a space-efficient probabilistic data structure that tests whether an element is a member of a set.

questions.question: What is database partitioning?
options: Dividing database into smaller, manageable pieces
questions.explanation: Database partitioning divides a large database into smaller, more manageable parts while maintaining logical unity.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Docker?
questions.explanation: Docker is a platform for developing, shipping, and running applications in isolated containers.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Kubernetes?
questions.explanation: Kubernetes is an open-source container orchestration platform for automating deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications.

questions.question: What is the ACID property in databases?
questions.explanation: ACID properties ensure reliable transaction processing: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability.

questions.question: What is the BASE property in NoSQL?
options: Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent
questions.explanation: BASE is an alternative to ACID, emphasizing availability over consistency: Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a content delivery network (CDN)?
options: Cache and deliver content from edge locations
questions.explanation: CDN caches content at geographically distributed edge servers to reduce latency and improve performance.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a distributed lock?
options: Coordinate access to shared resources in distributed systems
questions.explanation: Distributed locks ensure that only one process can access a shared resource at a time across multiple machines.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a reverse proxy cache?
questions.explanation: A reverse proxy cache stores responses from backend servers to reduce load and improve response times.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a health check?
questions.explanation: Health checks monitor the status of services to ensure they are running correctly and can handle requests.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a data warehouse?
questions.explanation: A data warehouse is a central repository of integrated data from multiple sources, optimized for analysis and reporting.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a data lake?
options: Store raw data in native format
questions.explanation: A data lake stores vast amounts of raw data in its native format until it's needed for analysis.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a message broker?
questions.explanation: A message broker is middleware that translates messages between formal messaging protocols, enabling service communication.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a service registry?
options: Track available service instances and their locations
questions.explanation: A service registry maintains a list of available service instances and their network locations for service discovery.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a dead letter queue?
options: Store messages that couldn't be processed
questions.explanation: A dead letter queue stores messages that couldn't be successfully processed for later analysis or reprocessing.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a connection pool?
options: Reuse database connections to improve performance
questions.explanation: Connection pooling maintains a cache of database connections that can be reused, reducing the overhead of creating new connections.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a write-ahead log (WAL)?
options: Ensure durability by logging changes before applying them
questions.explanation: WAL ensures durability by writing changes to a log before applying them to the database, enabling recovery after crashes.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a consistent hashing?
options: Distribute data evenly across nodes with minimal reorganization
questions.explanation: Consistent hashing minimizes data movement when nodes are added or removed in a distributed system.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a gossip protocol?
questions.explanation: Gossip protocol is a peer-to-peer communication mechanism where nodes periodically exchange state information with random peers.

questions.question: What is the purpose of an idempotent API?
options: Ensure same request produces same result multiple times
questions.explanation: An idempotent API ensures that making the same request multiple times has the same effect as making it once, crucial for reliability.

questions.question: What is the output of: typeof null?
questions.explanation: This is a known JavaScript quirk. typeof null returns 'object' due to a bug in the original JavaScript implementation.

questions.question: What does the 'this' keyword refer to in an arrow function?
options: The lexical scope where it was defined
questions.explanation: Arrow functions don't have their own 'this' binding. They inherit 'this' from the enclosing lexical scope.

questions.question: Which method is used to add elements to the end of an array?
questions.explanation: The push() method adds one or more elements to the end of an array and returns the new length.

questions.question: What is a closure in JavaScript?
options: A function that returns another function
A function with access to variables from its outer scope
A way to close browser windows
A method to end program execution
questions.explanation: A closure is a function that has access to variables in its outer (enclosing) lexical scope, even after the outer function has returned.

questions.question: What is the difference between '==' and '==='?
options: == performs type coercion, === checks type and value
questions.explanation: == performs type coercion before comparison, while === (strict equality) checks both type and value without coercion.

questions.question: What is hoisting in JavaScript?
options: Variable and function declarations are moved to the top of their scope
questions.explanation: Hoisting is JavaScript's behavior of moving declarations to the top of their scope before code execution.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'use strict'?
options: Enable strict mode to catch common coding errors
questions.explanation: 'use strict' enables strict mode, which catches common coding mistakes and prevents certain actions.

questions.question: What is the event loop in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: The event loop is a mechanism that handles the execution of multiple chunks of code over time, managing the call stack and callback queue.

questions.question: What is a Promise in JavaScript?
options: An object representing eventual completion or failure of an async operation
questions.explanation: A Promise is an object that represents the eventual completion (or failure) of an asynchronous operation and its resulting value.

questions.question: What does 'async/await' do?
options: Provides syntactic sugar for working with Promises
questions.explanation: async/await is syntactic sugar built on top of Promises, making asynchronous code look and behave more like synchronous code.

questions.question: What is the difference between 'let', 'const', and 'var'?
questions.explanation: var is function-scoped and can be redeclared; let is block-scoped and can be reassigned; const is block-scoped and cannot be reassigned.

questions.question: What is destructuring in JavaScript?
options: Extracting values from arrays or objects into variables
questions.explanation: Destructuring is a syntax that allows unpacking values from arrays or properties from objects into distinct variables.

questions.question: What is the spread operator (...)?
questions.explanation: The spread operator (...) expands an iterable (like an array) into individual elements, useful for copying and merging.

questions.question: What is the rest parameter?
options: Collects remaining arguments into an array
questions.explanation: The rest parameter (...) collects all remaining arguments into an array, allowing functions to accept variable numbers of arguments.

questions.question: What is a callback function?
options: A function passed as an argument to another function
questions.explanation: A callback is a function passed as an argument to another function, to be executed later (often after an async operation).

questions.question: What is the difference between null and undefined?
options: null is assigned, undefined means not assigned
undefined is assigned, null means not assigned
questions.explanation: undefined means a variable has been declared but not assigned a value; null is an assigned value representing 'no value'.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'bind()' method?
options: Create a new function with a specific 'this' context
questions.explanation: bind() creates a new function with a specified 'this' value and optional initial arguments.

questions.question: What is the difference between 'call()' and 'apply()'?
options: call() takes arguments separately, apply() takes an array
questions.explanation: call() invokes a function with arguments provided individually, while apply() takes arguments as an array.

questions.question: What is a higher-order function?
options: A function that takes or returns another function
questions.explanation: A higher-order function is a function that takes one or more functions as arguments or returns a function.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'map()' method?
options: Transform each element of an array and return a new array
questions.explanation: map() creates a new array by calling a function on every element of the original array.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'filter()' method?
options: Create a new array with elements that pass a test
questions.explanation: filter() creates a new array with all elements that pass the test implemented by the provided function.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'reduce()' method?
options: Reduce array to a single value by applying a function
questions.explanation: reduce() executes a reducer function on each element, resulting in a single output value.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'forEach()' method?
options: Execute a function for each array element
questions.explanation: forEach() executes a provided function once for each array element, but doesn't return a new array.

questions.question: What is the difference between 'for...in' and 'for...of'?
options: for...in iterates over keys, for...of iterates over values
questions.explanation: for...in iterates over enumerable property names (keys), while for...of iterates over iterable object values.

questions.question: What is a template literal?
options: String literals with embedded expressions using backticks
questions.explanation: Template literals use backticks (`) and allow embedded expressions with ${}, multi-line strings, and string interpolation.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'Object.freeze()'?
questions.explanation: Object.freeze() makes an object immutable - you cannot add, delete, or modify its properties.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'Object.seal()'?
options: Prevent adding/removing properties but allow modification
questions.explanation: Object.seal() prevents adding or removing properties, but existing properties can still be modified.

questions.question: What is prototypal inheritance?
options: Objects inherit from other objects via prototype chain
questions.explanation: JavaScript uses prototypal inheritance where objects can inherit properties and methods from other objects through the prototype chain.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'Symbol' in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: Symbol is a primitive data type that creates unique, immutable identifiers, often used as object property keys.

questions.question: What is a generator function?
options: A function that can pause and resume execution
questions.explanation: Generator functions (function*) can pause execution with yield and resume later, useful for iterators and async operations.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'WeakMap'?
options: A Map with weak references to keys
questions.explanation: WeakMap holds weak references to keys, allowing garbage collection if there are no other references to the key object.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'WeakSet'?
options: A Set with weak references to objects
questions.explanation: WeakSet holds weak references to objects, allowing them to be garbage collected if no other references exist.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'Proxy' in JavaScript?
options: Intercept and customize operations on objects
questions.explanation: Proxy allows you to create a proxy for another object, intercepting and redefining fundamental operations.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'Reflect' API?
options: Provide methods for interceptable JavaScript operations
questions.explanation: Reflect provides methods for interceptable JavaScript operations, often used with Proxy for meta-programming.

questions.question: What is event bubbling?
options: Events propagating from child to parent elements
questions.explanation: Event bubbling is when an event propagates from the target element up through its ancestors in the DOM tree.

questions.question: What is event capturing?
options: Events propagating from parent to child elements
questions.explanation: Event capturing (trickling) is when an event propagates from the root down to the target element.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'preventDefault()'?
options: Prevent the default action of an event
questions.explanation: preventDefault() prevents the default action associated with an event (like form submission or link navigation).

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'stopPropagation()'?
options: Stop event from bubbling up or capturing down
questions.explanation: stopPropagation() prevents the event from propagating (bubbling or capturing) to other elements.

questions.question: What is the difference between 'innerHTML' and 'textContent'?
options: innerHTML parses HTML, textContent treats as plain text
questions.explanation: innerHTML parses and renders HTML tags, while textContent treats everything as plain text and is safer against XSS.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'localStorage'?
options: Store key-value pairs in browser with no expiration
questions.explanation: localStorage stores key-value pairs in the browser with no expiration time, persisting even after browser restart.

questions.question: What is the difference between 'localStorage' and 'sessionStorage'?
options: localStorage persists, sessionStorage clears on tab close
questions.explanation: localStorage persists data indefinitely, while sessionStorage clears data when the browser tab is closed.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'JSON.stringify()'?
options: Convert JavaScript object to JSON string
questions.explanation: JSON.stringify() converts a JavaScript object or value to a JSON string.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'JSON.parse()'?
options: Parse JSON string to JavaScript object
questions.explanation: JSON.parse() parses a JSON string and constructs the JavaScript value or object described by the string.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'setTimeout()'?
options: Execute code after a specified delay
questions.explanation: setTimeout() executes a function or code after a specified delay in milliseconds.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'setInterval()'?
options: Execute code repeatedly at specified intervals
questions.explanation: setInterval() repeatedly executes a function or code at specified time intervals until cleared.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'clearTimeout()'?
options: Cancel a timeout set by setTimeout()
questions.explanation: clearTimeout() cancels a timeout previously established by calling setTimeout().

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'clearInterval()'?
options: Cancel an interval set by setInterval()
questions.explanation: clearInterval() cancels a timed, repeating action previously established by calling setInterval().

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'fetch()' API?
options: Make HTTP requests and return Promises
questions.explanation: fetch() provides a modern interface for making HTTP requests and returns a Promise that resolves to the Response.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'async' keyword?
options: Declare a function that returns a Promise
questions.explanation: The async keyword declares an asynchronous function that implicitly returns a Promise.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'await' keyword?
questions.explanation: await pauses async function execution until the Promise is resolved, then returns the resolved value.

questions.question: What is Node.js?
options: A JavaScript runtime built on Chrome's V8 engine
questions.explanation: Node.js is a JavaScript runtime environment that executes JavaScript code outside a web browser.

questions.question: Which module is used to create a web server in Node.js?
questions.explanation: The 'http' module is used to create HTTP servers and handle HTTP requests and responses.

questions.question: What is npm?
questions.explanation: npm (Node Package Manager) is the default package manager for Node.js used to install and manage dependencies.

questions.question: What is the purpose of package.json?
questions.explanation: package.json contains project metadata, dependencies, scripts, and configuration for Node.js projects.

questions.question: Which method is used to read a file asynchronously in Node.js?
questions.explanation: fs.readFile() reads files asynchronously with a callback, while fs.readFileSync() is synchronous.

questions.question: What is Express.js?
options: A web application framework for Node.js
questions.explanation: Express.js is a minimal and flexible Node.js web application framework for building web and mobile applications.

questions.question: What is middleware in Express.js?
options: Functions that execute during request-response cycle
questions.explanation: Middleware functions have access to request and response objects and can execute code, modify them, or end the cycle.

questions.question: What is the Event Loop in Node.js?
questions.explanation: The Event Loop is what allows Node.js to perform non-blocking I/O operations despite JavaScript being single-threaded.

questions.question: What is the purpose of process.env?
questions.explanation: process.env is an object containing the user environment variables, commonly used for configuration.

questions.question: Which module is used for path operations in Node.js?
questions.explanation: The 'path' module provides utilities for working with file and directory paths.

questions.question: What is a callback function in Node.js?
options: A function passed as argument to be executed later
questions.explanation: A callback is a function passed as an argument to another function to be executed after an operation completes.

questions.question: What is the purpose of require() in Node.js?
questions.explanation: require() is used to import modules, JSON files, and local files in Node.js applications.

questions.question: What is module.exports used for?
options: Make functions/objects available to other files
questions.explanation: module.exports is used to export functions, objects, or values from a module to be used in other files.

questions.question: What is the difference between readFile and readFileSync?
options: readFile is async, readFileSync is sync
questions.explanation: readFile() is asynchronous (non-blocking), while readFileSync() is synchronous (blocking).

questions.question: What is a stream in Node.js?
options: Objects for reading/writing data in chunks
questions.explanation: Streams are objects that let you read data from a source or write data to a destination in a continuous manner.

questions.question: Which are the types of streams in Node.js?
questions.explanation: Node.js has four types of streams: Readable, Writable, Duplex (both), and Transform (modify data).

questions.question: What is Buffer in Node.js?
questions.explanation: Buffer is a temporary storage area for binary data, used when working with streams and file operations.

questions.question: What is the purpose of __dirname?
options: Absolute path of directory containing current file
questions.explanation: __dirname is a global variable that contains the absolute path of the directory containing the current file.

questions.question: What is the purpose of __filename?
questions.explanation: __filename contains the absolute path of the currently executing file including the filename.

questions.question: What is cluster module used for?
options: Create child processes to utilize multiple CPU cores
questions.explanation: The cluster module allows you to create child processes (workers) that share server ports to utilize multi-core systems.

questions.question: What is the purpose of process.nextTick()?
options: Execute callback after current operation completes
questions.explanation: process.nextTick() defers execution of a function until the next iteration of the event loop.

questions.question: What is the difference between setImmediate() and process.nextTick()?
options: nextTick executes before I/O, setImmediate after
questions.explanation: process.nextTick() executes before I/O events, while setImmediate() executes after I/O events in the event loop.

questions.question: What is REPL in Node.js?
questions.explanation: REPL is an interactive shell that processes Node.js expressions, useful for testing and debugging.

questions.question: What is the purpose of util.promisify()?
questions.explanation: util.promisify() converts callback-based functions to promise-based functions for easier async/await usage.

questions.question: What is EventEmitter in Node.js?
questions.explanation: EventEmitter is a class that facilitates communication between objects in Node.js using events.

questions.question: How do you emit an event in Node.js?
questions.explanation: Use emitter.emit('eventName', args) to emit an event from an EventEmitter instance.

questions.question: What is the purpose of child_process module?
questions.explanation: child_process module allows you to spawn child processes to execute system commands or run other programs.

questions.question: What is the difference between spawn() and exec()?
options: spawn streams data, exec buffers data
questions.explanation: spawn() returns a stream for large data, while exec() buffers output and returns it all at once.

questions.question: What is the purpose of crypto module?
questions.explanation: The crypto module provides cryptographic functionality including hashing, HMAC, cipher, decipher, and more.

questions.question: What is the purpose of os module?
questions.explanation: The os module provides operating system-related utility methods and properties like platform, CPU, memory info.

questions.question: What is the purpose of querystring module?
options: Parse and format URL query strings
questions.explanation: The querystring module provides utilities for parsing and formatting URL query strings.

questions.question: What is the purpose of url module?
questions.explanation: The url module provides utilities for URL resolution and parsing.

questions.question: What is middleware chaining in Express?
options: Execute multiple middleware functions in sequence
questions.explanation: Middleware chaining allows multiple middleware functions to be executed in order using next().

questions.question: What is the purpose of body-parser middleware?
questions.explanation: body-parser middleware parses incoming request bodies (JSON, URL-encoded) and makes it available under req.body.

questions.question: What is CORS in Node.js?
questions.explanation: CORS is a mechanism that allows restricted resources on a web page to be requested from another domain.

questions.question: What is the purpose of helmet middleware?
options: Secure Express apps by setting HTTP headers
questions.explanation: Helmet helps secure Express apps by setting various HTTP headers to protect against common vulnerabilities.

questions.question: What is JWT?
questions.explanation: JWT (JSON Web Token) is a compact, URL-safe means of representing claims to be transferred between two parties.

questions.question: What is the purpose of morgan middleware?
questions.explanation: Morgan is an HTTP request logger middleware for Node.js that logs request details.

questions.question: What is the purpose of dotenv package?
questions.explanation: dotenv loads environment variables from a .env file into process.env for configuration management.

questions.question: What is the purpose of nodemon?
options: Automatically restart server on file changes
questions.explanation: Nodemon automatically restarts the Node.js application when file changes are detected during development.

questions.question: What is the purpose of async/await in Node.js?
options: Write asynchronous code in synchronous style
questions.explanation: async/await provides a cleaner syntax for working with promises, making asynchronous code look synchronous.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Promise.all()?
questions.explanation: Promise.all() takes an array of promises and returns a single promise that resolves when all promises resolve.

questions.question: What is the purpose of try-catch in async/await?
questions.explanation: try-catch blocks are used with async/await to handle errors from rejected promises.

questions.question: What is the purpose of mongoose?
questions.explanation: Mongoose is an ODM (Object Data Modeling) library for MongoDB and Node.js that provides schema-based modeling.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Sequelize?
questions.explanation: Sequelize is a promise-based ORM for Node.js that supports PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, and MSSQL.

questions.question: What is the purpose of socket.io?
questions.explanation: Socket.io enables real-time, bidirectional and event-based communication between browser and server.

questions.question: What is the purpose of passport.js?
questions.explanation: Passport is authentication middleware for Node.js that supports various authentication strategies.

questions.question: What is the purpose of multer?
questions.explanation: Multer is a middleware for handling multipart/form-data, primarily used for uploading files.

questions.question: What is the purpose of joi?
questions.explanation: Joi is a powerful schema description language and data validator for JavaScript objects.

questions.question: What is the purpose of winston?
questions.explanation: Winston is a versatile logging library for Node.js that supports multiple transports and log levels.

questions.question: What is React?
options: A JavaScript library for building user interfaces
questions.explanation: React is a JavaScript library developed by Facebook for building user interfaces, especially single-page applications.

questions.question: What is JSX?
questions.explanation: JSX is a syntax extension for JavaScript that allows you to write HTML-like code in JavaScript files.

questions.question: What is a component in React?
questions.explanation: A component is a reusable, independent piece of UI that can be composed to build complex interfaces.

questions.question: What are the two types of components in React?
questions.explanation: React has Functional components (using functions) and Class components (using ES6 classes).

questions.question: What is state in React?
questions.explanation: State is a built-in object that stores property values belonging to a component and can change over time.

questions.question: What are props in React?
questions.explanation: Props (properties) are arguments passed into React components, similar to function parameters.

questions.question: What is the difference between state and props?
options: State is mutable, props are immutable
questions.explanation: State is managed within the component and can change, while props are passed from parent and are read-only.

questions.question: What is the useState hook?
options: Hook to add state to functional components
questions.explanation: useState is a Hook that lets you add React state to functional components.

questions.question: What is the useEffect hook?
options: Hook for side effects in functional components
questions.explanation: useEffect lets you perform side effects in functional components (data fetching, subscriptions, DOM updates).

questions.question: What is the Virtual DOM?
questions.explanation: Virtual DOM is a lightweight copy of the actual DOM that React uses to optimize updates and improve performance.

questions.question: What is reconciliation in React?
questions.explanation: Reconciliation is the process React uses to diff the Virtual DOM and update only changed parts of the actual DOM.

questions.question: What is the purpose of keys in React lists?
questions.explanation: Keys help React identify which items have changed, been added, or removed, optimizing list rendering.

questions.question: What is the useContext hook?
questions.explanation: useContext lets you subscribe to React context without introducing nesting in your component tree.

questions.question: What is React Context?
options: Way to pass data through component tree
questions.explanation: Context provides a way to pass data through the component tree without passing props manually at every level.

questions.question: What is the useRef hook?
options: Hook to create mutable ref object
questions.explanation: useRef returns a mutable ref object that persists for the lifetime of the component, useful for DOM access.

questions.question: What is the useMemo hook?
questions.explanation: useMemo returns a memoized value, recomputing only when dependencies change, optimizing performance.

questions.question: What is the useCallback hook?
questions.explanation: useCallback returns a memoized callback function, preventing unnecessary re-renders of child components.

questions.question: What is the useReducer hook?
questions.explanation: useReducer is an alternative to useState for managing complex state logic, similar to Redux reducers.

questions.question: What is React Router?
options: Library for routing in React applications
questions.explanation: React Router is a standard library for routing in React, enabling navigation between different views.

questions.question: What is the purpose of componentDidMount?
options: Lifecycle method called after component mounts
questions.explanation: componentDidMount is invoked immediately after a component is mounted, ideal for API calls and subscriptions.

questions.question: What is the purpose of componentWillUnmount?
questions.explanation: componentWillUnmount is called before a component is unmounted, used for cleanup like canceling timers.

questions.question: What is the purpose of shouldComponentUpdate?
questions.explanation: shouldComponentUpdate lets you control whether a component should re-render, optimizing performance.

questions.question: What is React.memo?
questions.explanation: React.memo is a higher-order component that memoizes functional components to prevent unnecessary re-renders.

questions.question: What is prop drilling?
questions.explanation: Prop drilling is passing props through multiple component levels to reach a deeply nested component.

questions.question: What is Redux?
questions.explanation: Redux is a predictable state container for JavaScript apps, commonly used with React for state management.

questions.question: What are the three principles of Redux?
options: Single source of truth, State is read-only, Changes via pure functions
questions.explanation: Redux follows: single source of truth (one store), state is read-only, and changes are made with pure functions.

questions.question: What is an action in Redux?
questions.explanation: Actions are plain JavaScript objects that have a type field describing what happened in the application.

questions.question: What is a reducer in Redux?
options: Pure function that returns new state
questions.explanation: A reducer is a pure function that takes previous state and an action, and returns the next state.

questions.question: What is the Redux store?
questions.explanation: The store is an object that brings actions and reducers together and holds the entire state tree.

questions.question: What is the purpose of dispatch in Redux?
questions.explanation: dispatch() is a function that sends actions to the Redux store to trigger state changes.

questions.question: What is middleware in Redux?
questions.explanation: Middleware provides a way to extend Redux with custom functionality, intercepting actions before they reach reducers.

questions.question: What is Redux Thunk?
questions.explanation: Redux Thunk is middleware that allows you to write action creators that return functions instead of actions.

questions.question: What is the purpose of connect() in React-Redux?
options: Connect React components to Redux store
questions.explanation: connect() is a function that connects a React component to the Redux store, providing state and dispatch.

questions.question: What is the useSelector hook in React-Redux?
options: Hook to extract data from Redux store
questions.explanation: useSelector allows you to extract data from the Redux store state using a selector function.

questions.question: What is the useDispatch hook in React-Redux?
questions.explanation: useDispatch returns a reference to the dispatch function from the Redux store.

questions.question: What is React.Fragment?
options: Wrapper to group elements without extra DOM node
questions.explanation: React.Fragment lets you group a list of children without adding extra nodes to the DOM.

questions.question: What is the purpose of React.StrictMode?
questions.explanation: StrictMode is a tool for highlighting potential problems, activating additional checks and warnings in development.

questions.question: What is lazy loading in React?
questions.explanation: Lazy loading defers loading of components until they're needed, improving initial load time.

questions.question: What is React.lazy()?
questions.explanation: React.lazy() lets you render a dynamic import as a regular component, enabling code-splitting.

questions.question: What is Suspense in React?
options: Component to show fallback while loading
questions.explanation: Suspense lets you specify a loading indicator while waiting for lazy-loaded components to load.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Error Boundaries?
options: Catch JavaScript errors in component tree
questions.explanation: Error Boundaries are React components that catch JavaScript errors anywhere in their child component tree.

questions.question: What is the purpose of getDerivedStateFromError?
options: Update state after error is thrown
questions.explanation: getDerivedStateFromError is a lifecycle method used in error boundaries to update state after an error.

questions.question: What is the purpose of componentDidCatch?
questions.explanation: componentDidCatch is used in error boundaries to log error information and perform side effects.

questions.question: What is controlled component in React?
options: Form element controlled by React state
questions.explanation: A controlled component is a form element whose value is controlled by React state via props and onChange.

questions.question: What is uncontrolled component in React?
options: Form element that maintains its own state
questions.explanation: An uncontrolled component stores its own state internally and uses refs to access current values.

questions.question: What is the purpose of React.createRef()?
options: Create ref object to access DOM nodes
questions.explanation: React.createRef() creates a ref object that can be attached to React elements via the ref attribute.

questions.question: What is forwardRef in React?
questions.explanation: forwardRef lets components pass a ref through to a child component, useful for reusable component libraries.

questions.question: What is the purpose of React.Children?
questions.explanation: React.Children provides utilities for dealing with the opaque data structure of props.children.

questions.question: What is the purpose of React.cloneElement()?
options: Clone and return new React element
questions.explanation: React.cloneElement() clones an element and returns a new element with new props merged in.

questions.question: What is the purpose of React.isValidElement()?
options: Check if object is a React element
questions.explanation: React.isValidElement() verifies that an object is a React element, returning true or false.
